body.p:
The experienced XML developer writes lean, performant and simple code that others can easily understand. They master all areas of XML development and are familiar with publishing standards such as DocbookStandardsDocbook , DITAStandardsDITA and JATSStandardsJATS . They create beautiful PDF documents using XSL-FOXSL-FO and various page templates and have also worked in other application areas, such as the EDI Wikipedia page on Electronic Data Interchange / EDI Application AreasEDI domain. They are proficient with XML databases, such as MarklogicXML DatabaseMarkLogic or eXistXML DatabaseeXist and their query language XQueryProgramming LanguagesXQuery .
UWE test footnote (21/40, callout U): DITA fn maps to UWE footnote/key/desc.

The supreme discipline turns out to be complex XML-2-XML transformationsXML-2-XML Transformations. Particularly those that map from a relatively free content model to a restrictive content modelContent ModelRestrictive Content Model . These can take place outside and also inside an XML database - or be distributed via web requests.
It has proven effective to split such complex transformations into multiple steps, each of which forms a self-contained and easily testable unit.

Step by step, the XML input instance is transformed until the validatable result is finally produced. The XML of the intermediate steps usually cannot be validated against a schema, which is why special care is required during development.

section.title: Step-by-Step Python Script
section.p: During the development of a multi-stage transformation, you want to be able to easily review the intermediate steps. Another scripting language, such as PythonProgramming LanguagesPython , can help with this. The following script takes the XML data from an input folder, transforms it in sort order using the XSLT scripts located in the xslt folder, and writes the output clearly organized into the output folder.
section.pre:
import glob, os, shutil, getopt, sys, subprocess
SAXON_JAR = "/mnt/c/saxon/saxon9pe_98.jar" JAVA_CMD = "java"
def transform(): for fpath in glob.glob('input/*'): file_name= os.path.basename(fpath) input_folder = os.path.dirname(os.path.realpath(fpath)) input_file = os.path.join(input_folder, file_name)
steps = os.listdir("xslt") steps.sort() step = None
for step_file in steps: if not step_file.startswith("step"): continue step = step_file.split("_")[0] output_folder = input_folder.replace("input","output/"+step) current_step = os.path.join(output_folder, file_name) os.makedirs(output_folder,exist_ok=True) args = [ JAVA_CMD, "-classpath", SAXON_JAR, "net.sf.saxon.Transform", "-s:"+input_file, "-o:"+current_step, "-xsl:xslt/"+step_file, "filename="+os.path.basename(input_file).replace(".xml","") ] try: subprocess.call(args) except: print ("ERROR: Could not transform file: "+fpath+" with: "+step_file) input_file = current_step print ("Transformed "+step+": "+fpath)
transform() print ("Done")
section.p: The script can of course be extended with additional functionality, such as a validation for the final step or a delta comparison of intermediate results with those of the previous transformation run.

If you want to take this even further, you can also use a BPMN engineBusiness Process ManagementCamunda BPMN Engine
Process Management, such as Camunda Camunda (a specialized task executor for Camunda, made specifically for these XML purposes, can also be found in my Github repository tektur_worker).

section.title: Patterns for Recurring Steps
section.p: A multi-stage transformation that maps to a restrictive content model perhaps works like a gold panning pipeline, where content is sifted and shaken until the expected result is obtained.
The following patterns for recurring steps can be identified:

section.title: Marking Elements
section.p: Transformation PatternsMarking If you want to transform everything at once, you quickly run into trouble. It is advisable to first mark elements and then, in the next step, perform specific operations on the marked elements.
Here, an artificial attribute @copy-target-id with a value from @copy-id is set on an outline element, which in the following step is copied to the position after @copy-id.

section.title: Copying Elements
section.p: Transformation PatternsCopying A reusable step that copies elements marked with @copy-target-id to a position marked with @copy-id could look like this, for example:
section.pre:
<xsl:stylesheet xmlns:xsl="http://www.w3.org/1999/XSL/Transform" xmlns:xs="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema" exclude-result-prefixes="xs" version="2.0">
<xsl:key name="targets" match="*[@copy-target-id]" use="@copy-target-id"/>
<!-- copy elements from src to target -->
<xsl:template match="*[@copy-id]"> <xsl:copy> <xsl:apply-templates select="node()|@*"/> <xsl:apply-templates select="key('targets',@copy-id)" mode="copied"/> </xsl:copy> </xsl:template>
<!-- remove original position and attributes -->
<xsl:template match="*[@copy-target-id]"/> <xsl:template match="@copy-target-id|@copy-id" mode="copied"/>
<xsl:template match="node()|@*" mode="#all"> <xsl:copy> <xsl:apply-templates select="node()|@*" mode="#current"/> </xsl:copy> </xsl:template>
</xsl:stylesheet>
section.p: With such an approach, you can successively restructure the XML instance through individual copy steps and track the intermediate results. Such an exploratory approach has enormous advantages when you are not yet entirely clear about the algorithm.

section.title: Pulling Elements Up
section.p: Transformation PatternsPulling Up If a deeply nested element does not fit in the target DTD position, you can "pull it up" with the following templates:
section.pre:
<xsl:template match="table[descendant::table or descendant::ol]"> <xsl:copy> <xsl:apply-templates mode="remove-table-ol"/> </xsl:copy> <xsl:apply-templates select="descendant::ol | descendant::table"/> </xsl:template>
<xsl:template match="*[self::ol or self::table]" mode="remove-table-ol"/>
section.p: Here, tables and lists within a table are placed after the table. Using a mode (see also an example of using modes here: Mode vs. Tunnel Solution , these nodes are "cut" from the target XML tree.

section.title: Demarcating Blocks
section.p: Transformation PatternsDemarcating Blocks If block structures need to be bracketed, for example when in the HTML chapter they are only denoted by h1 heading tags, then a template like this may help:
Using the XPath fn:is() function, the generate-id() comparison could even be further shortened.

section.title: Wrapping Mixed Content
section.p: Transformation PatternsWrapping Mixed Content Very unpleasant is sporadically occurring XML mixed content, e.g. between paragraphs. The following templates can handle this. First, you can mark the mixed content in a preceding step and wrap it in an artificial paragraph ...
... here marked with @content="mixed". In the following step, the original paragraphs, which are now nested inside the artificial paragraph, are unwrapped:
